nativeRange.habitat: Found in shallow muddy and sandy substrates near reefs. The anemone buries its column in the soft sediment, leaving its tentacles and oral disc exposed at or near the surface.

decorPreferences: open sandy area away from rockwork

diet.notes: Photosynthetic via zooxanthellae symbiotes (primary energy source). Target-feeding is generally advised weekly with meaty foods such as silversides, krill, mysis shrimp, small pieces of fish, and mussel. Feed directly to the oral disc using tongs or a feeding stick; overfeeding has been known to cause regurgitation and stress.

goodWith: Amphiprion perideraion (pink skunk anemonefish — common host)
non-aggressive reef fish kept well away from tentacles
cleaner shrimp (with caution — tentacles may sting shrimp)
avoidWith: other anemone species (will sting and kill neighbors)
LPS and SPS corals placed within wandering range
small invertebrates that may wander into tentacles
angelfish and butterflyfish known to nip anemones
powerheads and intakes without adequate guards

imageCandidates.notes: Dan Schofield, 2048x1538. Sharp in-situ side profile on dark sand clearly showing the diagnostic long corkscrew tentacles (white curled tips over green tentacles) and exposed oral disc; cleanest ID-card image of the species. Faint corner attribution text only.
Dan Schofield, 2048x1601. Vibrant top-down view showing green tentacle bases with white corkscrew tips radiating from the oral disc; different angle for variety, sharp and well-lit.

Dan Schofield, 2048x1536. Wide in-habitat shot of a large colourful specimen (purple/green/pink flowing tentacles) hosting a clownfish plus dascyllus and cardinalfish; illustrates its role as a clownfish host in a display context.

summary: The Long Tentacle Anemone (Macrodactyla doreensis) is a well-known clownfish host from Indo-Pacific sandy shallows, recognized by its long corkscrew tentacles and vivid red-orange column base. It is an intermediate-care species that requires a deep sand bed, moderate to high light, and weekly target-feeding.
careNotes:
Following a systematic revision of stichodactylid anemones in 2023, Macrodactyla doreensis was reclassified under the name Radianthus doreensis, though both names remain in use in the hobby.

Substrate requirement: The LTA is a sand-substrate dweller and generally will not thrive without a proper sand bed. A minimum of 10 cm (approximately 4 inches) of sand or fine rubble is needed so the anemone can bury its column. Without adequate substrate the anemone tends to wander the tank in search of a suitable spot, potentially contacting and stinging corals or getting pulled into powerhead intakes.

Wandering behavior: When unhappy with lighting, flow, or substrate, the LTA will detach and move. This is one of the more common causes of specimen loss — a wandering anemone can be shredded by pumps. All powerheads and return pumps should have foam or mesh guards.

Lighting: Generally placed in the lower to middle zone of the aquarium despite moderate-to-high light requirements, because the anemone lives on the sand bed naturally. Estimated 150–300 PAR at sand level is generally advised; the anemone will often orient its disc toward the most suitable light source.

Clownfish hosting: The LTA is one of the well-known clownfish hosts kept in reef aquariums. Amphiprion clarkii (Clark's anemonefish) and A. perideraion (pink skunk anemonefish) are the most commonly documented associations in aquaria; A. ocellaris and A. chrysopterus have also been reported as hosts. Dascyllus trimaculatus (three-spot dascyllus) has been documented associating with the LTA in the wild. Most clownfish will be protective of their host and may occasionally harass other tankmates.

Aggression: The LTA has a potent sting capable of injuring or killing nearby invertebrates and small fish that wander into its tentacles. In a reef tank, adequate space (30+ cm) should be left around the anemone's likely territory, with the understanding that a wandering specimen can kill corals on contact.

Feeding: Target-feeding weekly with meaty foods (silversides, krill, mysis, small fish pieces, mussel) is generally advised. The anemone should close around the food and not regurgitate within 1–2 hours; regurgitation often indicates the piece was too large or the animal is stressed.

Iodine and trace element supplementation is noted in some care guides as beneficial for anemone health over the long term.

The lower end of the recommended temperature range (23°C) may reflect transport and acclimation tolerances rather than optimal long-term husbandry conditions.
breedingNotes: Captive reproduction of Long Tentacle Anemones has not been documented as a reproducible practice in the aquarium hobby. Anemones can reproduce asexually by fission (splitting) under favorable conditions, though this tends to occur spontaneously rather than being induced by hobbyists. Sexual reproduction involves broadcast spawning on natural reefs.

additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Stichodactylidae, order Actiniaria), 2023 reclassification to Radianthus doreensis, native range (Japan south through Papua New Guinea to northern Australia), depth range (typically ≤5 m), habitat description (shallow muddy substrates), and full list of clownfish hosts (A. clarkii, A. ocellaris, A. perideraion, A. chrysopterus, Dascyllus trimaculatus). Not used for husbandry parameters.
